ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'fried chicken' class; no specific entry for Malay spiced fried chicken breast (ayam berempah). Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried chicken meat generically; no specific berempah spice variant exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for ayam berempah or Malay spiced fried chicken. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.description: 1 piece of chicken breast with sambal
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 150 g, consisting of one piece of deep-fried spiced chicken breast accompanied by a portion of burnt green chili sambal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, fried (FDC ID 171477) combined with HPB Singapore nutrient data for spiced fried chicken and sambal components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients for the chicken breast component are anchored to USDA FDC ID 171477 (fried chicken breast, meat only). Sodium, spice-blend contributions, and sambal components are adapted using HPB Singapore nutrient composition data for spiced fried chicken dishes. The input per_100g values (energy 266.67 kcal, protein 19.44 g, fat 17.78 g, carbohydrate 6.67 g) are consistent with a composite of fried chicken breast plus a green chili sambal and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are not available from the composite source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on fried chicken and low-carbohydrate spiced meat dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ayam berempah breast with burnt green chili sambal. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low category) based on analogous fried chicken and spiced meat dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and published international GI tables. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with modest carbohydrate (6.67 g/100g) from spice coating and sambal; the spice blend (turmeric, cumin, coriander) may modestly lower postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (40 × 10.0 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 416.67 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 625 mg sodium, representing 27% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g and request reduced salt in the berempah marinade; pairing with potassium-rich sides such as banana or leafy greens may partially offset the sodium load per DASH diet principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is modest at 6.67 g/100g with a low estimated GI of approximately 40; however, deep-frying adds substantial fat (17.78 g/100g) which may impair insulin sensitivity over time.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a moderate portion (100–150 g) as the low carbohydrate content and high protein (19.44 g/100g) support glycaemic stability, but should avoid pairing with white rice and request grilled or baked preparation to reduce fat-driven insulin resistance risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes total fat 17.78 g/100g including saturated fat 4.44 g/100g, trans fat 0.11 g/100g, and cholesterol 83.33 mg/100g; sodium at 416.67 mg/100g further elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this deep-fried preparation due to elevated saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium; requesting a grilled or baked version of ayam berempah would substantially reduce the atherogenic and hypertensive risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; sodium at 416.67 mg/100g exceeds the RA sodium threshold and worsens joint tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this deep-fried preparation due to AGE formation; a grilled or baked version retaining the anti-inflammatory turmeric and ginger spice blend would be preferable, and salt should be reduced to bring sodium below 400 mg/100g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Berempah Breast with Burnt Green Chili Sambal is a Malaysian-Singaporean dish consisting of a chicken breast marinated in a fragrant blend of ground spices — typically turmeric, cumin, coriander, lemongrass, galangal, garlic, and ginger — then deep-fried until golden and crispy. It is served with a distinctive burnt green chili sambal (sambal hijau bakar), made by charring or pan-frying green chilies with shallots, garlic, and aromatics to produce a smoky, mildly spicy condiment. The chicken breast yields a juicy interior with a well-seasoned, aromatic crust, while the sambal adds a smoky heat and umami depth. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 267 kcal, 19.4 g protein, 17.8 g fat (including 4.4 g saturated fat and 0.11 g trans fat), 6.7 g carbohydrate, 2.2 g sugar, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and 417 mg sodium. The high protein content supports satiety and glycaemic stability, while the spice blend — particularly turmeric and ginger — offers modest anti-inflammatory properties. However, the deep-frying method elevates total fat, saturated fat, and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making this dish a concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease, hypertension, and rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is halal-certified, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as a main course at lunch or dinner, often paired with steamed rice or nasi lemak in Malaysian and Singaporean hawker and restaurant settings.

confidence_reason: Core macros are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for fried chicken breast and are adapted with HPB Singapore data for the spiced and sambal components. No exact approved-source entry exists for ayam berempah breast with burnt green chili sambal as a composite dish; values are retained from the input at medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are unavailable from the composite source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite ayam berempah breast with burnt green chili sambal dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in available approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed micronutrient data for this composite dish are not available in the approved sources consulted. Potassium estimated at 280 mg/100g based on USDA FDC data for fried chicken breast and aromatic ingredients. Input ethnic_tags ('Laksa', 'Nasi Lemak', 'Beverage') were erroneous and have been corrected to reflect the actual dish. Duplicate and inconsistent portion entries in the input (multiple 'is_default: true' entries including 'chicken leg' portions for a breast dish) have been rationalised; the canonical serving is set to 150 g (1 piece of chicken breast with sambal). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (40 × 10.0 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.0. Trans fat 0.11 g/100g is a low but non-zero value consistent with deep-frying in vegetable oil.
